# **PROJECT 2: VLAN Segmentation Lab**

#### **By: Cydrick James Cortel**

## **Scenario**

A small company wants to segment their network for security and performance. Different departments should be on separate broadcast domains, but still able to communicate through a central router when needed. 

## **Network Requirements**

**Company has 3 departments requiring network segmentation:**  
   
1\. **Sales Department (VLAN 10\)**  
   \- Needs: 20 devices  
   \- Network: 192.168.10.0/24  
   \- Description: Sales team, customer-facing  
   
2\. **Engineering Department (VLAN 20\)**  
   \- Needs: 15 devices  
   \- Network: 192.168.20.0/24  
   \- Description: Developers, sensitive intellectual property  
   
3\. **Guest Network (VLAN 30\)**  
   \- Needs: 10 devices  
   \- Network: 192.168.30.0/24  
   \- Description: Visitors, contractors, untrusted devices

# **Network Topology Diagram**

![image alt](https://github.com/Cyy26/networking-labs/blob/main/02-vlan-segmentation/screenshots/Network%20Topology.png?raw=true)

**SW1 connects to:**  
\- Sales PCs (VLAN 10\) on ports Fa0/0-0/9  
\- Engineering PCs (VLAN 20\) on ports Fa0/10-0/19  
   
**SW2 connects to:**  
\- Guest PCs (VLAN 30\) on ports Fa0/0-0/9  
\- Additional Sales/Engineering if needed

# **Connections Table**

| Devices | Connections | Outgoing Port | Device Connected | Connected Port |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Router 0 | Straight-Through | Gigabit 0/0 | Switch0 | Gigabit 0/1 |
| Switch 0 | Straight-Through | Gigabit 0/2 | Switch 1 | Gigabit 0/1 |
| PC 0 | Straight-Through | Fa0/0 | Switch 0 | Fa0/1 |
| PC1 | Straight-Through | Fa0/0 | Switch 0 | Fa0/2 |
| PC2 | Straight-Through | Fa0/0 | Switch 0 | Fa0/10 |
| PC3 | Straight-Through | Fa0/0 | Switch 0 | Fa0/11 |
| PC4 | Straight-Through | Fa0/0 | Switch 1 | Fa0/2 |
| PC5 | Straight-Through | Fa0/0 | Switch 1 | Fa0/1 |

# **IP Addressing Table**

| Device | Interface | IP Address | Default Gateway | VLAN | Network Address | Broadcast Address |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Router0 | G0/0.10 | 192.168.10.1/24 |  | VLAN 10 | 192.168.10.0 | 192.168.10.255 |
|  | G0/0.20 | 192.168.20.1/24 |  | VLAN 20 | 192.168.20.0 | 192.168.20.255 |
|  | G0/0.30 | 192.168.30.1/24 |  | VLAN 30 | 192.168.30.0 | 192.168.30.255 |
| Switch0 | VLAN10 |  |  |  |  |  |
| Switch0 | VLAN20 |  |  |  |  |  |
| Switch1 | VLAN30 |  |  |  |  |  |
| PC0 | NIC | 192.168.10.2/24 | 192.168.10.1 | VLAN10 | 192.168.10.0 | 192.168.10.255 |
| PC1 | NIC | 192.168.10.3/24 | 192.168.10.1 | VLAN10 | 192.168.10.0 | 192.168.10.255 |
| PC2 | NIC | 192.168.20.2/24 | 192.168.20.1 | VLAN20 | 192.168.20.0 | 192.168.20.255 |
| PC3 | NIC | 192.168.20.3/24 | 192.168.20.1 | VLAN20 | 192.168.20.0 | 192.168.20.255 |
| PC4 | NIC | 192.168.30.2/24 | 192.168.30.1 | VLAN30 | 192.168.30.0 | 192.168.30.255 |
| PC5 | NIC | 192.168.30.3/24 | 192.168.30.1 | VLAN30 | 192.168.30.0 | 192.168.30.255 |

# **Configuration Steps**

**Step 1: VLAN Configuration**

**Switch0 Commands:**  
enable  
conf t  
vlan 10  
name sales  
exit

vlan 20  
name engineering  
exit

vlan 30  
name guest  
exit

int fa0/1  
switchport mode access  
switchport access vlan 10  
exit

int fa0/2  
switchport mode access  
switchport access vlan 10  
exit

int fa0/10  
switchport mode access  
switchport access vlan 20  
exit

int fa0/11  
switchport mode access  
switchport access vlan 20  
exit

**Switch1 Commands:**  
enable  
configure terminal

vlan 10  
name sales  
exit

name vlan 20  
name engineering  
exit

name vlan 30  
name guest  
exit

int fa0/1  
switchport mode access  
switchport access vlan 30  
exit

int fa0/2  
switchport mode access  
switchport access vlan 30  
exit

**Step 2: Trunk Configuration**  
**Switch0 \<-\> Switch1**  
**Switch 0 Config:**  
int ga0/2  
switchport mode trunk  
switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30

**Switch 1 Config:**  
int gig0/1  
switchport mode trunk  
switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30  
exit

**Switch 0 \<-\> Router (router-to-switch)**  
**Router0 Config:**  
int ga0/1  
switchport mode trunk  
switchport trunk allowed vlan 10,20,30

**Step 3: Inter-VLAN Routing**  
**Router0 Commands:**

enable  
configure terminal

int g0/0  
no shutdown  
exit

int g0/0.10  
encapsulation dot1Q 10  
ip address 192.168.10.1 255.255.255.0  
exit	

int g0/0.20  
encapsulation dot1Q 20  
ip address 192.168.20.1 255.255.255.0  
exit

int g0/0.30  
encapsulation dot1Q 30  
ip address 192.168.30.1 255.255.255.0

**Step 4: ACL Configuration**  
**Router Config:**

access-list 100 deny ip 192.168.30.0 0.0.0.255 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255  
access-list 100 deny ip 192.168.30.0 0.0.0.255 192.168.20.0 0.0.0.255  
access-list 100 permit ip any any

int g0/0.30  
ip access-group 100 in

**The implemented ACL provides unidirectional isolation:**

**VLAN Segmentation:** VLAN provides logical network separation without physical infrastructure changes. This is critical for security and and performance in modern networks 

❌ \- Guest network CANNOT access the Sales or Engineering networks (security requiremen)  
✅- Sales and Engineering can access Guest network (operational requirement)

This is standard practice in enterprise network where:  
\- Guest traffic is untrusted and must be isolated from internal resources  
\- IT Staff need access to guest network for support and monitoring  
\- Internal users may need shared guest resources (printers, collaboration tools)

**Verification:**

\- Guest \-\> Sales: Request timed out (ACL Blocking)  
\- Guest \-\> Engineering: Request timed out (ACL Blocking)  
\- Sales \-\> Guest: Reply received (ACL Blocking)  
\- Engineering \-\> Guest: Reply received (ACL Blocking)

# **Challenges Encountered**

One of the challenges that I encountered is the configuration for the default gateway of each vlans. It slipped into my mind that this is a router-on-a-stick. Also, the one thing that made me stuck is the configuration of ACL and the concept behind it. Because of this, I tried researching on how ACL works and where do you use it.

# **Key Learnings**

**VLAN Segmentation:** VLAN provide logical network segmentation without physical infrastructure changes. This is useful for security and performance in modern networks  
**Trunk Configuration:** Trunks can carry multiple VLANS between switches using 802.1Q tagging. Must be configured on both side of the link.  
**Router-on-a-stick:** Single router interface with subinterfaces dedicated to each network allowing inter-vlan routing without multiple physical router interfaces. Efficient for small/medium networks.  
**Access Control Lists:** ACLs filter traffic based on source/destination IPs. Order matters (deny rule first, then permit). Applied per interface, per direction..  
**Troubleshooting Methodology:**

* Verify layer by layer (can PC reach gateway, can gateway reach destination)  
* Use show commands to check for misconfiguration (show vlan, show trunk, show ip interface brief)  
* Document issues and solutions for future reference


**Real-world applicability:** This lab mirrors actual enterprise network segmentation requirements.The skills translate directly to help desk, network technician, and junior administrator roles.

## **Lab Files**

\- \`network-topology.pkt\` \- Packet Tracer file with complete working configuration  
\- \`screenshots/\` \- Verification command outputs and ping tests  
\- \`configs/\` \- Full running-config from each device

**Note:** .pkt file available in repository. Load in Cisco Packet Tracer 7.3+ to explore the configuration interactively.
